Paper #1: Resource-Efficient Emulation of Majorana Zero Mode Braiding on a Superconducting Trijunction ​

Authors: Rahul Singh, Weixin Lu, Kaelyn Ferris, Javad Shabani
Affiliations: UC Santa Barbara, NYU, IBM Quantum
arXiv: 2603.03645 [quant-ph]
Date: March 4, 2026
Domain: superconductivity, quantum-info
Why collected: Resource-efficient quantum simulation of Majorana braiding — directly relevant to NISQ-era topological quantum computing

Summary ​

This work presents a resource-efficient method to emulate Majorana zero mode (MZM) braiding in a superconducting trijunction geometry on gate-based quantum processors. By constructing explicit braiding operators that act directly in the Majorana operator algebra (rather than using adiabatic evolution), the authors achieve significantly reduced circuit depths and two-qubit gate counts compared to conventional Trotterized adiabatic approaches. The protocol is validated numerically, confirming the expected non-Abelian braiding statistics with relative phase Δϕ≈π/2 for single braids.

Core Concepts ​

  • Majorana Zero Modes (MZMs): Self-conjugate fermionic excitations (γ=γ†) that obey non-Abelian statistics, enabling topologically protected quantum gates.

  • Kitaev Chain: A 1D p-wave superconductor model hosting MZMs at its ends in the topological phase. The Hamiltonian is:

    H=i2∑j[−μγjxγjy+(t+|Δ|)γjyγj+1x+(−t+|Δ|)γjxγj+1y]

    where μ is on-site energy, Δ is the superconducting gap, and t is hopping amplitude.

  • Topological vs Trivial Phases (in the Δ=t regime):

    • Trivial: t=0, μ>0 — no unpaired Majoranas
    • Topological: t>0, μ=0 — MZMs localized at chain ends

  • Braiding Protocol: A six-step process where arms of the trijunction cyclically transition between topological and trivial phases, causing two MZMs to exchange positions.

  • Exchange Operators: The fundamental building blocks for braiding:

    Oklmp=12(1+γkmγlp)

    satisfying Oklmpγkm(Oklmp)†=−γlp and Oklmpγlp(Oklmp)†=γkm.

  • Coupler-Based Jordan-Wigner Transformation: A modified JW mapping introducing an auxiliary qubit C at the junction:

    γaj(a)x=σCμσajx∏i=0j−1σaiz,γaj(a)y=σCμσajy∏i=0j−1σaiz

    where σC(1)≡σCx, σC(2)≡σCy, σC(3)≡σCz.

Key Innovations ​

  1. Direct Braiding Operators: Instead of adiabatic evolution under time-dependent Hamiltonians, the authors construct explicit unitary operators that implement the same trijunction-to-trijunction transformations directly in the Majorana algebra. Each braiding step decomposes into three sub-steps:

    • Phase change in donor arm (UcT): Converts topological to trivial phase
    • Junction coupling control (UacJ): Exchanges MZMs at the junction
    • Phase change in host arm (UaR): Converts trivial to topological phase

  2. Resource Efficiency: The braiding-operator approach achieves substantially lower two-qubit gate counts and circuit depths compared to adiabatic evolution with Trotterization, with favorable scaling as the number of sites per arm increases.

  3. Coupler-Based Mapping: A tailored Jordan-Wigner transformation that keeps operators local and facilitates interactions at the junction, particularly well-suited for IBM's heavy-hex qubit topology.

  4. Numerical Validation: Ground-state braiding phase analysis confirms the expected non-Abelian statistics with Δϕ≈π/2 for single braids and Δϕ≈π for double braids.

Technical Approach ​

System Hamiltonian: The trijunction Hamiltonian for arms a and b in topological phase, arm c in trivial phase:

Hab=iΔ∑k∈{a,b}∑j=0n−2γkjyγk,j+1x+iα∑l=0n−1γclxγcly+iϵabctabγa0xγb0x

where α=−μ/2 and ϵabc is the Levi-Civita symbol.

Braiding Operator Construction: Each of the six braiding steps consists of three exchange sub-operators:

  • Translation step (UcT): Products of Oc(n−2−j),c(n−1−j)yy operators
  • Junction step (UacJ): Oc0,a0yyOc0,a0xx acting on nearest sites
  • Rotation step (UaR): Products of Oa(j+1),a(j)yy operators

For a single-site trijunction (n=1), UcT and UaR reduce to identity, and each step is implemented solely by UacJ.

Qubit Mapping: The Hamiltonian maps to Pauli operators via the coupler-based JW transformation:

Hab=−Δ∑k∈{a,b}∑j=0n−2σkjxσk,j+1x−α∑l=0n−1σclz−ϵabctabσC(c)σa0xσb0x

Main Results ​

Circuit Resource Comparison (Fig. 6):

  • For n≥2 sites per arm, the braiding-operator protocol requires significantly fewer ECR (two-qubit) gates than adiabatic evolution
  • The advantage grows with system size due to more favorable scaling

Circuit Depth (Fig. 7):

  • Coupler-based mapping yields substantially shallower circuits than continuous-index mapping
  • Particularly advantageous for NISQ devices where gate errors accumulate with circuit depth

Ground-State Braiding Phase Verification:

  • Single braid (t=0 to t=3τ): Relative phase Δϕ≈π/2 between ground states
  • Double braid (Ubraid2): Relative phase Δϕ≈π
  • Consistent with expected Majorana braiding operator UAB=exp⁡(π4γAγB)

Fidelity Trade-off (Fig. 5):

  • Adiabatic evolution requires more Trotter steps for higher fidelity, leading to rapidly growing circuit depth
  • Braiding operators avoid this trade-off by construction

Reading Notes ​

  • [ ] Mathematical Detail: The explicit construction of braiding operators from exchange operators (Eqs. 11-13) and their composition into the full protocol deserves careful study.

  • [ ] Physical Interpretation: Understanding how the three sub-steps (translation, junction coupling, rotation) physically move the MZMs through the trijunction.

  • [ ] Implementation Considerations: The coupler-based mapping is designed for heavy-hex topology — how does this constrain the implementation on different quantum hardware?

  • [ ] Comparison with Prior Work: The paper cites Ref. [26] (Stenger et al.) which used adiabatic evolution on IBM quantum computers — understanding the quantitative improvement in resource requirements.

  • [ ] Scalability: The protocol scales linearly with the number of sites per arm — investigating the practical limits for near-term devices.

  • [ ] Experimental Feasibility: What are the specific error thresholds required for observing the braiding phase on current NISQ hardware?

Paper #2: Anomalous Periodicity and Parafermion Hybridization in Superconducting Qubits ​

Authors: Alessio Calzona, Matteo Carrega, Luca Chirolli
Affiliations: University of Würzburg, CNR-SPIN Genova, UC Berkeley
arXiv: 2208.07408 [cond-mat.mes-hall]
Date: August 15, 2022
Domain: superconductivity, quantum-info
Why collected: Parafermions as a generalization of Majorana zero modes — prediction of distinctive 4mπ periodicity and microwave spectroscopic signatures

Summary ​

This work investigates parafermions (PFs) — a generalization of Majorana zero modes with larger topological degeneracy — in a hybrid platform combining fractional quantum Hall (FQH) states with superconducting qubits. The authors propose a parafermionic fluxonium circuit and predict striking signatures: (1) anomalous 4mπ periodicity in the qubit spectrum due to the fractional Josephson effect, and (2) distinctive microwave spectroscopy features that reveal different parafermion coupling mechanisms. The study opens pathways for detecting parafermions and characterizing their hybridization through accessible superconducting circuit measurements.

Core Concepts ​

  • Parafermions (PFs): Generalization of Majorana zero modes from Z2 to Zp (p=2m) anyons. They satisfy:

    αip=1,αip−1=αi†,αiαj=ei2π/pαjαi(i<j)

    The Fock space for a pair of Zp PFs is p-dimensional (vs 2-dimensional for MZMs).

  • Platform: FQH system in ν=1/m Laughlin state with two superconducting leads. Four PFs (α0,α1,α2,α3) localize at the SC lead ends.

  • Bosonization Description: The system is described by Luttinger liquid fields ϕ and θ with commutation [ϕ(x),θ(x′)]=i(π/m)Θ(x−x′). The PFs are represented as:

    α1(2)=eiπm(N^L(R)+N^c),α0(3)=eiπm(N^L(R)+N^bl(r))

  • Parafermion Coupling at Junction:

    HPF(0)=∑k=1p/2γ(k)cos⁡(kϕ+2πn^pp)

    where γ(k) describes tunneling of charge ke/m quasiparticles between SC leads. This yields the 2pπ-periodic fractional Josephson effect.

  • Off-Diagonal Couplings: Through SC leads, PFs can hybridize via:

    HPF(1)=∑k=1p/2λ(k)α1k+H.c.

    where λ(k) corresponds to 2kπ phase slip and charge ke/m tunneling.

  • Parafermionic Fluxonium: The Hamiltonian combines the fluxonium terms with PF coupling:

    H=12ECn^ϕ2+EL(ϕ−2πΦ/Φ0)2−EJcos⁡(ϕ)⏟Hfluxonium+HPF(0)+HPF(1)

Key Innovations ​

  1. Anomalous Periodicity: The parafermion-mediated fractional Josephson effect leads to 4mπ periodicity of the qubit spectrum as a function of external flux (e.g., 12π for m=3). This is a striking signature distinguishing PFs from MZMs (4π).

  2. Spectroscopic Signatures: Different PF hybridization mechanisms (λ(k) terms) produce recognizable patterns in the microwave spectrum:

    • λ(1): Lifts all crossings, restores 2π-periodicity
    • λ(2): Protected crossings at specific flux values, 4π-periodicity
    • λ(3): Further protected crossings, 6π-periodicity (for m=3)

  3. Tunable Couplings: The proposal envisions using a scanning gate microscope (SGM) tip to locally gate the FQH region, enabling active control of parafermion couplings through antidot-enhanced resonant tunneling.

  4. Detection Scheme: The microwave spectrum detected by a nearby resonator provides clear evidence of parafermion physics, including the anomalous periodicity and coupling-specific anticrossing patterns.

Technical Approach ​

Effective Hamiltonian: In the combined basis |nϕ,np⟩ (fluxon number and PF number), the low-energy Hamiltonian is:

Heff=EL2(2πnϕ−2πΦ/Φ0)2−ES2∑a=1p(Tϕ+ei2πp(nϕ+np)|np⟩⟨np+a|+H.c.)

where ES is the effective Josephson energy and Tϕ± are fluxon tunneling operators.

Coupling Matrices: The off-diagonal couplings λ(k) are represented by generalized Pauli matrices in the p-dimensional PF subspace:

  • λ(1): Matrix with all entries equal to 1 (like σx)
  • λ(2): Matrix with entries e2πi/p on specific off-diagonals
  • λ(3): Matrix with entries e4πi/p on further off-diagonals

Physical Origin: The different λ(k) couplings arise from higher-order tunneling processes of fractional quasiparticles through the superconducting lead, with amplitudes scaling as λ(k)∝λk in perturbative regime.

Main Results ​

Spectrum Periodicity (Fig. 1b-d):

  • For m=3, the spectrum shows 12π periodicity as a function of Φ/Φ0
  • Each PF number state np=0,…,5 corresponds to a shifted parabolic branch
  • Level crossings occur at specific flux values determined by the balance between inductive and Josephson energies

Effect of Off-Diagonal Couplings (Fig. 2):

  • λ(1)=0.015ωp: All crossings lifted, spectrum becomes 2π-periodic
  • λ(2)=0.01ωp: Protected crossings remain, 4π-periodicity
  • λ(3)=0.01ωp: More protected crossings, 6π-periodicity

Microwave Spectrum (Fig. 3, Fig. S4):

  • Without spurious couplings: Minima slightly shifted from (2k−1)π by ∼γ/EL
  • With λ(1): Large splitting at Φ/Φ0=π
  • With λ(2): Splitting around Φ/Φ0∼0.9π
  • With λ(3): Splitting around Φ/Φ0∼0.82π

Protection Assessment: Finite PF coupling reduces the full degeneracy of the non-Abelian manifold, but partial protection remains. The fluxonium qubit provides a tool to assess the remaining degree of protection.

Reading Notes ​

  • [ ] Bosonization Formalism: Understanding the Luttinger liquid description of FQH edge states and how the sine-Gordon potential pins the ϕ field to minima ϕ=πN^μ/m.

  • [ ] Instanton Calculation: The amplitudes γ(k) and λ(k) are calculated via instanton techniques — worth studying the detailed derivation in the references [28, 38, 39, 78].

  • [ ] Comparison with Majorana: How does the parafermion fluxonium reduce to the Majorana case when m=1? What are the key differences in the spectral signatures?

  • [ ] Experimental Realization: What are the specific requirements for the FQH-SC hybrid platform? What FQH filling factors and SC materials are most promising?

  • [ ] SGM Implementation: How precisely can a scanning gate microscope tip control the parafermion couplings? What are the spatial resolution and tuning range requirements?

  • [ ] Beyond Nearest-Neighbor: The paper mentions possible three- or four-PF couplings — how would these manifest in the spectrum and what physical processes could generate them?

Paper #3: Signatures of Parafermion Zero Modes in Fractional Quantum Hall-Superconductor Heterostructures ​

Authors: Junyi Cao, Angela Kou, Eduardo Fradkin
Affiliations: University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign
arXiv: 2309.14411 [cond-mat.str-el]
Date: September 25, 2023 (v2: March 27, 2024)
Domain: superconductivity, quantum-info
Why collected: Current-phase relation signatures of parafermions in FTSC Josephson junctions — complementary to spectroscopic approaches, addressing the role of backscattering

Summary ​

This work investigates parafermion zero modes (PZMs) in fractional topological superconductors (FTSCs) formed by proximitizing ν=1/m fractional quantum Hall (FQH) edges with s-wave superconductors. Focusing on a Josephson junction comprising two such FTSCs, the authors analyze the interplay between parafermion tunneling, Cooper pair tunneling, and backscattering. A key finding is that while the ideal 4mπ-periodic fractional Josephson effect reduces to 4π-periodic (or 2π-periodic at finite temperature) in the presence of backscattering, distinctive features in the current-phase relation persist as signatures of parafermion physics. The authors also propose a parity-projected measurement scheme to recover the temperature-dependent 4π-periodic fractional Josephson effect.

Core Concepts ​

  • Fractional Topological Superconductor (FTSC): A hybrid system of FQH edge states (ν=1/m) proximity-coupled to an s-wave superconductor. The bosonized Hamiltonian is:

    H=∫−L0dx[mvF4π((∂xϕR)2+(∂xϕL)2)+mU2π∂xϕR∂xϕL−Δℓ02cos⁡[m(ϕR−ϕL)]]

  • Bosonization Variables: Fields ϕ=12(ϕR+ϕL) (charge) and ϑ=12(ϕR−ϕL) (superconducting phase) with commutation [ϕ(x),ϑ(x′)]=iπmΘ(x−x′).

  • Sine-Gordon Theory: In the (ϕ,ϑ) basis:

    H=∫−L0dx2π[K(∂xϕ)2+m2K(∂xϑ)2−2πΔℓ02cos⁡(2mϑ)]

    with Luttinger parameter K=vF+UvF−U.

  • Parafermion Zero Mode: Localized at the FTSC end:

    α0=1b∫−b0dxeiϑ(x)∝eiϑ0

    satisfying [H,α0]=0 and P^α0=eiπ/mα0P^.

  • Quasiparticle Parity: A Z2m symmetry generator:

    P^≡exp⁡(iπQ^)=exp⁡[i(ϕ0−ϕ−L)]

    with P^−1ϑP^=ϑ+πm.

  • Josephson Junction: Two FTSCs with tunneling processes:

    • Parafermion tunneling: Charge e/m tunneling
    • Cooper pair tunneling: Charge 2e tunneling
    • Backscattering: Charge 0 (elastic scattering)

Key Innovations ​

  1. Effect of Backscattering: Backscattering explicitly breaks the Zm symmetry, reducing the 4mπ-periodic Josephson effect to 4π-periodic at T=0 and 2π-periodic at finite temperature (unless fermion parity is fixed). This explains why the ideal fractional Josephson effect may be difficult to observe.

  2. Persistent Signatures: Despite the reduced periodicity, the current-phase relation retains distinctive shape features that discriminate PZMs from MZMs:

    • Different dependence on tunneling amplitudes ΓP (parafermion) vs ΓΔ (Cooper pair)
    • Characteristic curvature and amplitude modulations

  3. Parity-Projected Measurement: A proposal to project onto fixed quasiparticle parity sectors using:

    P^r=12m∑k=02m−1ω−krΣ^k,ω=eiπ/m,Σ^=eiπn^(1)

    This recovers a temperature-dependent 4π-periodic fractional Josephson effect as a clear PZM signature.

  4. RG Analysis: Kosterlitz renormalization group analysis of the sine-Gordon theory yields the critical proximity gap:

    Δc≃mK−282mπ5/2forK>Kc=2m

    For ν=1 (m=1), the superconducting term is always relevant (Kc=2); for ν=1/3 (m=3), a finite Δc is required.

Technical Approach ​

Josephson Junction Hamiltonian:

HJ=−ΓPcos⁡(δϕSC2m)−ΓΔcos⁡(δϕSC)−ΓBcos⁡(2mϑ(1))

where δϕSC is the superconducting phase difference, ΓP is parafermion tunneling amplitude, ΓΔ is Cooper pair tunneling, and ΓB is backscattering.

Energy Spectrum: For n(1)−n(2)=r (fixed charge difference), the eigenenergies are:

Er=−ΓPcos⁡(δϕSC+2πr2m)−ΓΔcos⁡(δϕSC)

Current-Phase Relation:

I(δϕSC)=2eℏ∂E∂(δϕSC)=eΓPmℏsin⁡(δϕSC+2πr2m)+2eΓΔℏsin⁡(δϕSC)

Thermal Average:

⟨I⟩=∑rIr(δϕSC)e−βEr∑re−βEr

Main Results ​

Periodicity Reduction:

RegimePeriodicityCondition
Ideal (no backscattering)4mπΓB=0, fixed parity
With backscattering, T=04πΓB≠0
With backscattering, T>02πThermal averaging
Parity-projected, T>04πProjected measurement

Discriminating Features:

  • PZM vs MZM: Even with reduced periodicity, the detailed shape of I(ϕ) differs:
    • PZM: Higher harmonic content from 1/m factors
    • Different scaling with ΓP/ΓΔ ratio
    • Temperature dependence reveals underlying fractionalization

Critical Gap Estimate: For ν=1/3 (m=3) with K>Kc=2/3:

Δc∝(3K−2)

This indicates that proximity effect is not automatic in FQH systems — requires either attractive interactions or strong pairing.

Reading Notes ​

  • [ ] Bosonization Details: Understanding the Klein factors for quasiparticles vs electrons and their role in parafermion braiding (Appendix B).

  • [ ] Kosterlitz RG: Detailed derivation of the RG equations near the critical point and the phase boundary (Appendix C).

  • [ ] Projection Operators: Explicit construction of P^r in the clock model basis and their physical interpretation (Appendix D).

  • [ ] Comparison with Experiment: How do the theoretical predictions compare with the recent graphene CAR experiment [18]? What additional measurements would strengthen the PZM claim?

  • [ ] Universal Quantum Computation: PZMs enable Fibonacci anyons and universal TQC [9] — understanding the specific braiding properties required.

  • [ ] Higher-Order Tunneling: The paper mentions tunneling of groups of parafermions (e.g., m parafermions = Majorana) — when do these become relevant?

Paper #4: Majorana Zero Modes in the Lieb-Kitaev Model with Tunable Quantum Metric ​

Authors: Xingyao Guo, Xinglei Ma, Xuzhe Ying, K. T. Law
Affiliations: Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
arXiv: 2406.05789v2 [cond-mat.supr-con]
Date: November 23, 2024
Domain: superconductivity, flat-band, quantum-geometry
Why collected: First demonstration that the quantum metric (not just Berry curvature) sets a fundamental length scale controlling Majorana localization in flat-band superconductors

Summary ​

This work constructs a three-band Lieb-like lattice with an isolated flat band and introduces nearest-neighbor intra-orbital p-wave pairing to obtain the Lieb-Kitaev model. When the chemical potential lies within the flat band, the model hosts Majorana zero modes (MZMs) at the two ends of the 1D chain. Contrary to the naive expectation that MZMs in a flat band should be extremely localized (since the conventional BCS coherence length ξBCS→0), the authors show both numerically and analytically that the MZM localization length is governed by a new length scale set by the quantum metric of the flat band — the quantum metric length (QML) ξQM. The QML can be orders of magnitude longer than ξBCS, leading to long-range hybridization of the two end MZMs and ultra long-range crossed Andreev reflections (CARs).

Core Concepts ​

  • Lieb-like Lattice: A 1D lattice with three orbitals per unit cell (A,B,C) and on-site energies (−V,0,V). The normal-state Hamiltonian in Bloch basis c^(k)=(c^A,c^B,c^C)T is:

    h(k)=(−2tcos⁡(ka)−μ)I3+(−Vak0ak∗0ak∗0akV)

    where ak=−J(1+eika).

  • Flat Band: For t=0, the middle band is exactly flat with energy ϵ0=0 (at μ=0). The flat-band eigenstate is:

    |u0(k)⟩=1Nk(ak,V,−ak)T

  • Lieb-Kitaev Model: The BdG Hamiltonian with nearest-neighbor intra-orbital pairing:

    HBdG=∑kΨ^†(k)(h(k)−i2Δsin⁡(ka)I3i2Δsin⁡(ka)I3−h∗(−k))Ψ^(k)

    where Ψ^(k)=(c^(k),c^†(−k))T.

  • Quantum Metric Tensor: For the flat-band state,

    G(k)=⟨∂ku0(k)|[I−|u0(k)⟩⟨u0(k)|]|∂ku0(k)⟩

  • Quantum Metric Length (QML): The Brillouin-zone-averaged quantum metric defines a real-space length scale:

    ξQM≡∫02π/adk2πG(k)→V/J→02J4Va

    which diverges as V/J→0.

  • BCS Coherence Length: In the flat-band limit, the conventional coherence length vanishes:

    ξBCS≈atΔ→0(t→0)

  • Crossed Andreev Reflection (CAR): A nonlocal process where an electron incident on one lead is reflected as a hole in the other lead, mediated by the nonlocal MZM pair.

Key Innovations ​

  1. Quantum Metric Governs Majorana Localization: For the first time, the quantum metric (the real part of the quantum geometric tensor) is shown to control the real-space spread of MZMs. In the extreme flat-band limit where ξBCS→0, the MZM localization length is instead set by ξQM.

  2. Tunable QML: By adjusting V/J, the QML can be tuned from the lattice scale to values orders of magnitude larger. This provides a direct handle on MZM overlap without changing the pairing gap.

  3. Long-Range MZM Hybridization: When ξQM is comparable to the system length, the two end MZMs hybridize strongly even though the BCS coherence length is vanishingly small. This challenges the conventional intuition that flat bands always produce ultra-short localization lengths.

  4. Ultra Long-Range CAR: Because the MZMs can be nonlocal over distances ≫ξBCS, crossed Andreev reflections can remain significant even when the two metallic leads are separated by many lattice constants. The CAR amplitude can approach the maximal theoretical value.

Technical Approach ​

Model Construction:

  • Start from a spinless 1D Lieb-like lattice with three orbitals per unit cell.
  • Add a small next-nearest-neighbor intra-orbital hopping t to give the flat band a tiny dispersion.
  • Introduce nearest-neighbor intra-orbital p-wave pairing with amplitude Δ.
  • The topological phase is characterized by the Z2 invariant Q=sign(Pf[iH~(k=0)]Pf[iH~(k=π/a)])=−1 for |μ|<2t.

Analytical Derivation of QML:

  • From the flat-band eigenstate |u0(k)⟩, compute the quantum metric G(k).
  • Perform the Brillouin-zone average to obtain ξQM.
  • Show analytically that in the t→0 limit, the MZM wavefunction decay is governed by ξQM rather than ξBCS.

Numerical Verification:

  • Diagonalize the finite-size BdG Hamiltonian with open boundary conditions.
  • Extract the two lowest-energy states and verify their wavefunction spread scales with ξQM as V/J is varied.
  • Compute the CAR amplitude between two leads coupled to the end MZMs as a function of lead separation.

Main Results ​

Majorana Wavefunction Scaling (Fig. 2d):

  • For Δ=0.6t (extremely flat band), the MZM wavefunction penetrates much deeper into the bulk for smaller V/J (larger quantum metric).
  • The localization length extracted from numerical fits scales as ∼ξQM, confirming the analytical prediction.

QML vs BCS Coherence Length:

Parameter regimeDominant length scalePhysical consequence
V/J∼1ξQM∼aShort MZMs, Kitaev-like
V/J≪1ξQM≫a≫ξBCSLong MZMs, strong hybridization
t→0ξBCS→0 but ξQM finiteFlat-band MZMs remain delocalized

Crossed Andreev Reflection:

  • When lead separation L∼ξQM, the CAR amplitude remains large even for L≫ξBCS.
  • This establishes a novel transport fingerprint of quantum-metric-controlled MZMs.

Reading Notes ​

  • [ ] Quantum Geometric Tensor: Understanding the full quantum geometric tensor Gμν(k)=Gμν(k)−iFμν(k)/2 and why only the quantum metric (not Berry curvature) enters the MZM localization problem in this model.

  • [ ] Flat-Band Superconductivity: How does the vanishing Fermi velocity affect the standard BCS theory? Is the pairing still well-described by a mean-field gap, or are interaction effects more important?

  • [ ] Generalization: The authors claim the results generalize to other topological bound states — what would be the analogous QML for higher-order topological modes or parafermion zero modes?

  • [ ] Experimental Platform: Can the Lieb-Kitaev model be realized in cold-atom optical lattices or twisted bilayer systems where flat bands and tunable quantum metric are naturally present?

  • [ ] Relation to Our Blog Post: This paper uses a genuine Lieb lattice with a flat band, whereas our recent planar-Josephson-junction post is based on a 2D square-lattice altermagnet without a flat band. The two "Lieb" references are distinct and should not be conflated.

Issue status: 🔴 Closed (issue complete)
